title: What Can Landlords Claim CRA Landlord Tax Deductions Checklist: A Comprehensive Guide for Small Landlords
metaDescription: What can landlords claim CRA landlord tax deductions checklist: Learn about eligible expenses and deductions for your rental income tax return
content:
As a first-time landlord with a full-time day job, managing your rental properties and navigating the tax landscape can be overwhelming. One of the most critical aspects of being a landlord is understanding what can be claimed as CRA landlord tax deductions. The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) allows landlords to claim various expenses related to their rental properties to reduce their taxable income. In this article, we will explore the what can landlords claim CRA landlord tax deductions checklist, providing you with a comprehensive guide to help you maximize your deductions and minimize your tax liability.

## Introduction to CRA Landlord Tax Deductions
The CRA provides a detailed list of eligible expenses that landlords can claim as deductions on their tax returns. These expenses can be categorized into several groups, including operating expenses, capital expenditures, and financing costs. Understanding these categories and the specific expenses that fall under each is crucial for accurately completing your tax return. For more information on rental income tax in Canada, refer to our pillar guide: [Rental Income Tax in Canada: Complete Guide for Small Landlords (2026)](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/rental-income-tax-in-canada-complete-guide-for-small-landlords-2026).

## Operating Expenses: What Can Landlords Claim?
Operating expenses are the costs associated with the day-to-day management and maintenance of your rental property. These expenses can be claimed on Line 8710 of your T776 statement. Examples of operating expenses include:
* Advertising (Line 8520): $500 to advertise your rental property on online platforms
* Insurance (Line 8720): $1,200 for liability and property damage insurance
* Maintenance and repairs (Line 8960): $2,000 for plumbing and electrical repairs
* Utilities (Line 8830): $1,500 for water, gas, and electricity
* Property management fees (Line 8770): $3,000 for hiring a property management company

## Capital Expenditures: Depreciation and Amortization
Capital expenditures refer to the costs associated with acquiring or improving your rental property. These expenses can be claimed over several years through depreciation and amortization. For example, if you purchase a new appliance for your rental property at a cost of $1,000, you can claim 20% of the cost as a depreciation expense each year. The CRA provides a schedule of depreciation rates for various types of assets, including buildings, equipment, and fixtures.

## Financing Costs: Mortgage Interest and Property Taxes
Financing costs are the expenses associated with borrowing money to purchase or refinance your rental property. These expenses can be claimed as deductions on your tax return. Examples of financing costs include:
* Mortgage interest (Line 8710): $10,000 in interest on your rental property mortgage
* Property taxes (Line 8420): $3,500 in annual property taxes

## Common Mistakes Small Landlords Make
As a small landlord, it's easy to make mistakes when claiming CRA landlord tax deductions. Here are three common errors to avoid:
* Missing structural depreciation: Failing to claim depreciation on your rental property's building and structural components can result in a significant loss of deductions.
* Misclassifying line items: Incorrectly categorizing expenses as operating expenses or capital expenditures can lead to errors on your tax return.
* Inadequate record-keeping: Failing to maintain accurate and detailed records of your expenses can make it difficult to support your deductions in the event of a CRA audit.

For more information on year-end tax checklists and CRA-compliant bookkeeping, refer to our articles: [Year-End Tax Checklist for Canadian Landlords: A Comprehensive Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/year-end-tax-checklist-for-canadian-landlords-a-comprehensive-guide) and [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide).

## Key Takeaways
Here is a summary of the key takeaways from this article:
* Understand the different categories of expenses that can be claimed as CRA landlord tax deductions
* Accurately categorize and record your expenses to support your deductions
* Avoid common mistakes, such as missing structural depreciation and misclassifying line items
* Consider hiring a professional accountant or bookkeeper to help with your tax return
* If you're an Airbnb host, be sure to check out our article on [Airbnb Tax Rules: Canadian Landlords 2026](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/airbnb-tax-rules-canadian-landlords-2026)
